config.prompt: Name the U.S. state for each official nickname

items.explanation:
"Yellowhammer State" — named for the yellowhammer woodpecker (Northern Flicker), associated with Confederate soldiers from Alabama who wore yellow cloth on their uniforms
Capital: Montgomery; largest city: Birmingham
22nd state, admitted 1819
Montgomery was the first capital of the Confederate States (1861); site of the Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955–1956)

"The Last Frontier" — reflects Alaska's vast, remote, and largely unsettled wilderness
Capital: Juneau; largest city: Anchorage
49th state, admitted 1959
Largest U.S. state by area (665,384 sq mi) — more than twice the size of Texas
Purchased from Russia in 1867 for $7.2 million, initially mocked as "Seward's Folly"

"Grand Canyon State" — named for the Grand Canyon, one of the Seven Natural Wonders of the World
Capital and largest city: Phoenix
48th state, admitted 1912 (last of the contiguous states)
The Grand Canyon is 277 miles long, up to 18 miles wide, and over a mile deep

"The Natural State" — adopted in 1995, referring to the state's abundant lakes, rivers, mountains, and forests
Capital and largest city: Little Rock
25th state, admitted 1836
Previously nicknamed "The Land of Opportunity"; also known as "The Bear State" in the 19th century

"Golden State" — references the 1849 Gold Rush and the golden poppies that blanket the state's hillsides
Capital: Sacramento; largest city: Los Angeles
31st state, admitted 1850
Most populous U.S. state (~39 million); world's fifth-largest economy if counted as a country

"Centennial State" — achieved statehood in 1876, the centennial year of the Declaration of Independence
Capital and largest city: Denver
38th state, admitted 1876
Denver is the "Mile High City" — the State Capitol's west steps sit exactly 5,280 feet above sea level

"Constitution State" — refers to Connecticut's Fundamental Orders (1639), often called the first written democratic constitution
Capital: Hartford; largest city: Bridgeport
5th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
Also known as the "Nutmeg State," from Yankee peddlers allegedly selling fake wooden nutmegs

"First State" — Delaware was the first state to ratify the U.S. Constitution, on December 7, 1787
Capital: Dover; largest city: Wilmington
1st state, ratified 1787
Second-smallest state by area; known for business-friendly incorporation laws — over 60% of Fortune 500 companies are incorporated here

"Sunshine State" — adopted officially in 1970, reflecting the state's subtropical climate and tourism appeal
Capital: Tallahassee; largest city: Jacksonville (by area), Miami metro is largest by population
27th state, admitted 1845
Spanish explorer Juan Ponce de León named it "La Florida" (Land of Flowers) in 1513

"Peach State" — Georgia was once the top U.S. peach producer, though South Carolina now grows more
Capital and largest city: Atlanta
4th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
Named for King George II of Great Britain; Atlanta hosted the 1996 Summer Olympics

"Aloha State" — "aloha" is a Hawaiian word meaning love, affection, peace, and compassion
Capital and largest city: Honolulu (on Oahu)
50th state, admitted 1959
Only U.S. state composed entirely of islands; the only state located outside North America geographically

"Gem State" — Idaho produces 72 types of precious and semi-precious gemstones, more varieties than any other state
Capital and largest city: Boise
43rd state, admitted 1890
Also famous for potatoes — produces roughly one-third of the nation's potato crop

"Prairie State" — the tallgrass prairies that once covered most of the state before large-scale farming
Capital: Springfield; largest city: Chicago
21st state, admitted 1818
Abraham Lincoln lived in Springfield for 25 years; Chicago became a railroad hub and meatpacking center in the 19th century

"Hoosier State" — origin of "Hoosier" is debated; possibly from "Who's here?" called out by frontier settlers
Capital and largest city: Indianapolis
19th state, admitted 1816
Indianapolis hosts the Indy 500 (since 1911), the world's largest single-day sporting event by attendance

"Hawkeye State" — likely a tribute to Chief Black Hawk, the Sauk leader, or the fictional character in The Last of the Mohicans
Capital and largest city: Des Moines
29th state, admitted 1846
The Iowa caucuses have been the first major electoral event in the U.S. presidential nominating process since 1972

"Sunflower State" — the wild sunflower is the state flower, and Kansas is a major sunflower-producing state
Capital: Topeka; largest city: Wichita
34th state, admitted 1861
Geographic center of the contiguous U.S. is near Lebanon, Kansas; Dodge City was a legendary cattle town on the Santa Fe Trail

"Bluegrass State" — named for the bluegrass that grows in the fertile Bluegrass Region, prized for horse pasture
Capital: Frankfort; largest city: Louisville
15th state, admitted 1792
Home of the Kentucky Derby (since 1875), the oldest continuously held horse race in the U.S.; also known for bourbon whiskey

"Pelican State" — the brown pelican appears on the state flag and seal, symbolizing selfless nurturing
Capital: Baton Rouge; largest city: New Orleans
18th state, admitted 1812
Acquired as part of the Louisiana Purchase (1803); the only U.S. state that uses parishes instead of counties

"Pine Tree State" — roughly 89% of Maine is forested, the highest percentage of any U.S. state
Capital: Augusta; largest city: Portland
23rd state, admitted 1820 (separated from Massachusetts via the Missouri Compromise)
Easternmost state in the U.S.; Cadillac Mountain in Acadia National Park is one of the first places to see sunrise in the country

"Old Line State" — a tribute to Maryland's "troops of the line" who fought with distinction in the Revolutionary War; George Washington reportedly coined the term
Capital: Annapolis; largest city: Baltimore
7th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
Annapolis is home to the U.S. Naval Academy (founded 1845)

"Bay State" — named for the Massachusetts Bay Colony, founded in 1630 by Puritan settlers
Capital and largest city: Boston
6th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
Site of the opening battles of the American Revolution (Lexington and Concord, 1775); home to Harvard (1636), the oldest U.S. university

"Great Lakes State" — Michigan borders four of the five Great Lakes (Superior, Michigan, Huron, Erie)
Capital: Lansing; largest city: Detroit
26th state, admitted 1837
Also called the "Wolverine State"; Detroit became the center of the American auto industry in the early 20th century

"North Star State" — from the French motto "L'Étoile du Nord" (The Star of the North) on the state seal
Capital: St. Paul; largest city: Minneapolis
32nd state, admitted 1858
Also called "Land of 10,000 Lakes" — actually has 11,842 lakes of 10+ acres; source of the Mississippi River at Lake Itasca

"Magnolia State" — the magnolia is both the state tree and state flower, abundant throughout the state
Capital and largest city: Jackson
20th state, admitted 1817
Named for the Mississippi River (from Ojibwe "misi-ziibi," meaning "great river"); birthplace of the blues in the Delta region

"Show-Me State" — attributed to Congressman Willard Vandiver (1899): "I come from a state that raises corn and cotton... and I'm from Missouri. You have got to show me."
Capital: Jefferson City; largest city: Kansas City
24th state, admitted 1821
St. Louis was the gateway for westward expansion; the Gateway Arch (630 ft) commemorates this role

"Treasure State" — named for Montana's rich mineral deposits, including gold, silver, copper, and sapphires
Capital: Helena; largest city: Billings
41st state, admitted 1889
Site of the Battle of the Little Bighorn (1876), where Lakota and Cheyenne warriors defeated Custer's 7th Cavalry

"Cornhusker State" — references the state's massive corn production and the University of Nebraska's athletic teams
Capital: Lincoln; largest city: Omaha
37th state, admitted 1867
Only U.S. state with a unicameral (single-chamber) legislature; Omaha is home to Berkshire Hathaway (Warren Buffett)

"Silver State" — named for the massive silver deposits discovered in the Comstock Lode (1859)
Capital: Carson City; largest city: Las Vegas
36th state, admitted 1864 (during the Civil War, to support the Union cause)
Las Vegas is the entertainment and gambling capital of the U.S.; Nevada is the driest state by average annual rainfall

"Granite State" — named for the state's extensive granite formations and quarries
Capital: Concord; largest city: Manchester
9th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
Hosts the first-in-the-nation presidential primary; has no state income tax or sales tax

"Garden State" — possibly coined by Abraham Browning in 1876, referring to the state's fertile farmland and produce
Capital: Trenton; largest city: Newark
3rd state, ratified the Constitution in 1787
Most densely populated U.S. state; Thomas Edison's laboratory in Menlo Park earned it the nickname "Invention Factory"

"Land of Enchantment" — adopted in 1999, reflecting the state's dramatic landscapes, multicultural heritage, and artistic appeal
Capital: Santa Fe; largest city: Albuquerque
47th state, admitted 1912
Santa Fe is the oldest state capital in the U.S. (founded 1610); site of the first atomic bomb test at Trinity Site (1945)

"Empire State" — George Washington reportedly called New York the "Seat of the Empire," reflecting its wealth and influence
Capital: Albany; largest city: New York City
11th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
New York City served as the first U.S. capital (1789–1790); Ellis Island processed over 12 million immigrants (1892–1954)

"Tar Heel State" — from the state's colonial-era production of tar, pitch, and turpentine from its vast pine forests
Capital: Raleigh; largest city: Charlotte
12th state, ratified the Constitution in 1789
Site of the Wright Brothers' first powered flight at Kitty Hawk (1903)

"Peace Garden State" — named for the International Peace Garden on the U.S.–Canada border, dedicated in 1932
Capital: Bismarck; largest city: Fargo
39th state, admitted 1889
One of the least-populated states; the Bakken Formation oil boom transformed its economy in the 2010s

"Buckeye State" — named for the Ohio buckeye tree (Aesculus glabra), abundant in the state
Capital and largest city: Columbus
17th state, admitted 1803
Birthplace of seven U.S. presidents (Grant, Hayes, Garfield, B. Harrison, McKinley, Taft, Harding) — more than any other state except Virginia

"Sooner State" — "Sooners" were settlers who entered the Unassigned Lands before the official start of the 1889 Land Run
Capital and largest city: Oklahoma City
46th state, admitted 1907
Former Indian Territory; home to 39 tribal headquarters, the most of any U.S. state

"Beaver State" — the American beaver is the state animal, reflecting Oregon's fur-trading history
Capital: Salem; largest city: Portland
33rd state, admitted 1859
Crater Lake (1,943 ft deep) is the deepest lake in the U.S., formed in the caldera of Mount Mazama

"Keystone State" — Pennsylvania was the central ("keystone") colony among the original 13, both geographically and politically
Capital: Harrisburg; largest city: Philadelphia
2nd state, ratified the Constitution in 1787
Philadelphia hosted the Continental Congress and the Constitutional Convention; the Declaration of Independence was signed there (1776)

"Ocean State" — despite being the smallest U.S. state by area, Rhode Island has over 400 miles of coastline
Capital and largest city: Providence
13th state, ratified the Constitution in 1790 (last of the original 13)
Founded by Roger Williams in 1636 as a haven for religious freedom; Newport was a major colonial-era port

"Palmetto State" — named for the sabal palmetto tree; a palmetto-log fort on Sullivan's Island repelled a British attack in 1776
Capital and largest city: Columbia
8th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
First state to secede from the Union (December 1860); the Civil War began at Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor (April 1861)

"Mount Rushmore State" — home to Mount Rushmore, featuring the carved faces of Washington, Jefferson, T. Roosevelt, and Lincoln
Capital: Pierre; largest city: Sioux Falls
40th state, admitted 1889
The Crazy Horse Memorial, still under construction since 1948, will be the world's largest sculpture when completed

"Volunteer State" — from the large number of Tennessee volunteers who fought in the War of 1812 and the Mexican–American War
Capital and largest city: Nashville
16th state, admitted 1796
Nashville is the capital of country music; Memphis is the birthplace of rock and roll (Sun Studio) and home of Beale Street blues

"Lone Star State" — the single star on the Texas flag symbolizes independence, dating from the Republic of Texas (1836–1845)
Capital: Austin; largest city: Houston
28th state, admitted 1845
Second-largest state by both area and population; was an independent republic for nearly 10 years before joining the U.S.

"Beehive State" — the beehive symbolizes industry and hard work, values central to the Mormon pioneers who settled the territory
Capital and largest city: Salt Lake City
45th state, admitted 1896
Home to five national parks (the "Mighty Five"): Arches, Bryce Canyon, Canyonlands, Capitol Reef, and Zion

"Green Mountain State" — "Vermont" derives from French "vert mont" (green mountain), describing the Green Mountains running north–south through the state
Capital: Montpelier; largest city: Burlington
14th state, admitted 1791
Montpelier is the least-populous state capital (~8,000 people); Vermont produces more maple syrup than any other U.S. state

"Old Dominion" — Charles II of England gave Virginia this title for its loyalty to the Crown during the English Civil War
Capital: Richmond; largest city: Virginia Beach
10th state, ratified the Constitution in 1788
Birthplace of eight U.S. presidents, more than any other state; site of the first permanent English settlement at Jamestown (1607)

"Evergreen State" — named for the state's abundant evergreen forests, including Douglas fir, western red cedar, and Sitka spruce
Capital: Olympia; largest city: Seattle
42nd state, admitted 1889
Only state named after a U.S. president; Mount Rainier (14,411 ft) is the highest peak in the Cascades

"Mountain State" — the entire state lies within the Appalachian Mountains, with an average elevation of 1,500 feet
Capital and largest city: Charleston
35th state, admitted 1863 (split from Virginia during the Civil War)
The only state formed by seceding from a Confederate state; John Brown's raid on Harpers Ferry (1859) was a catalyst for the Civil War

"Badger State" — not from the animal, but from lead miners in the 1830s who dug into hillsides and lived in the tunnels like badgers
Capital: Madison; largest city: Milwaukee
30th state, admitted 1848
Also called "America's Dairyland"; leads the nation in cheese production and is home to the Green Bay Packers, the only community-owned NFL team

"Equality State" — Wyoming was the first U.S. territory to grant women the right to vote (1869) and the first state to elect a female governor (Nellie Tayloe Ross, 1925)
Capital and largest city: Cheyenne
44th state, admitted 1890
Also called the "Cowboy State"; Yellowstone (1872) was the world's first national park; least-populous U.S. state
